metadata.summary: Document splitting strategy for RAG: balance chunk size for embedding relevance vs context preservation.

content.title: Chunking Basics
content.summary: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller pieces for embedding and retrieval. The chunking strategy significantly impacts RAG performance - too large chunks dilute relevance, too small chunks lose context.

why.p: Effective chunking ensures that embedded text units contain enough context to be semantically meaningful while staying small enough to pass relevant results to the LLM. The trade-off between chunk size and context preservation directly affects retrieval quality and LLM reasoning accuracy.

list.item: Source documents are already structured records - no chunking needed
Content is code - use AST-based splitting rather than sentence/paragraph splitters
Documents are ultra-short (less than 200 tokens each) - chunking adds overhead
Legal or medical content requiring sentence-level precision - semantic chunking required

section.name: core-strategy
section.p: Chunking strategy must match content type. Markdown documents use header-based splitting. Code uses AST-based splitting. General text uses recursive or sentence splitting. This exploits document structure for better chunking and metadata preservation. Fixed-size and paragraph chunkers ignore token budgets; word counts diverge from token counts for non-English text. Sentence tokenizers (NLTK punkt) are English-centric; multilingual docs produce garbage splits. Overlap logic in recursive chunkers is character-based, not token-based - downstream models may still exceed context limits. No metadata is attached by default; source tracking must be wired in explicitly. Chunk-size decisions made without retrieval benchmarks frequently require re-indexing.

rule.statement: Start with chunk_size equals 500 tokens and overlap equals 50 tokens. Use 1000-1500 tokens for technical/legal. Use 200-400 tokens for QA content.
rule.rationale: 500 tokens is a practical middle ground that preserves context while keeping chunks focused. Adjust based on retrieval benchmarks - no theoretical size works universally.

rule.statement: Always measure chunk token counts with tiktoken before indexing. Word counts diverge significantly from token counts.
rule.rationale: Token counts directly impact embedding model input budgets and retrieval quality. Word counts are approximately 30 percent inaccurate for token budgets.

rule.statement: Set overlap to 10-20 percent of chunk_size. For QA corpora where questions span chunk boundaries use 20 percent.
rule.rationale: Overlap prevents abrupt context loss at chunk boundaries. Queries that span boundaries benefit from overlapping chunks.

section.name: fixed-size-chunking
section.p: Fixed-size chunking splits text into equal-length token units with overlap. Simple and predictable but ignores document structure.

example.title: Fixed-size chunking with token counting

section.name: sentence-based-chunking
section.p: Sentence-based chunking respects sentence boundaries while staying within size limits. More intelligent than fixed-size but still ignores deeper document structure.

example.title: Sentence-based chunking

section.name: paragraph-based-chunking
section.p: Paragraph-based chunking groups sentences by natural paragraph boundaries (double newlines), respecting document structure while staying within size constraints. Single paragraph that exceeds max_chunk_size is split by sentences. This approach preserves natural document flow better than sentence-by-sentence splitting.

example.title: Paragraph chunking with overflow handling

section.name: recursive-chunking
section.p: Recursive chunking applies multiple separators hierarchically: first split by paragraph, then by newline, then by sentence, then by word, recursing only when needed. Balances structure preservation with flexibility. Recursively splits text with separators, handling overlap at each level. If a chunk exceeds size with the current separator, the algorithm recurses to the next finer separator. This approach intelligently finds natural boundaries while respecting chunk size constraints.

example.title: Recursive chunking with multi-level separators

rule.statement: Attach source, page, chunk_index, and strategy metadata at creation time. Retrofitting metadata later requires full re-index.
rule.rationale: Metadata attached to chunks at creation enables source tracking, deduplication, and quality auditing downstream.

rule.statement: Use content-based IDs like md5(source_path + chunk_index) for chunk IDs, not sequential integers.
rule.rationale: Sequential IDs cause collisions when documents are updated or re-indexed. Content-based IDs enable idempotent re-ingestion.

rule.statement: Test chunking with at least five representative queries before committing to a strategy. Retrieval accuracy is the only ground truth.
rule.rationale: Theoretical chunking decisions rarely survive contact with real query patterns. Even 30 percent change in chunk size can dramatically affect retrieval.

rule.statement: For mixed corpora (documentation, code, chat), route each file type through its own chunker before merging into one index.
rule.rationale: A chunking strategy tuned for prose performs poorly on code and vice versa. Separate routing ensures each content type gets appropriate treatment.

rule.statement: Pre-clean text (strip headers/footers, normalize whitespace) before chunking. Noise propagates into all downstream chunks.
rule.rationale: Document headers, page numbers, and boilerplate become part of every chunk. Pre-cleaning prevents embedding model confusion.

rule.statement: Version the index name with the chunking config hash when strategy changes. Existing vector index entries become stale with new chunk sizes.
rule.rationale: Re-indexing with different chunk sizes invalidates relevance scores from the old index. Version tracking prevents accidental searches against mixed indices.

antipattern.description: Splitting mid-sentence and losing meaning at boundaries.
antipattern.reason: Abrupt sentence breaks create chunks that start or end mid-thought, confusing embedding models and reducing retrieval relevance.
Queries spanning chunk boundaries may fail to retrieve any relevant results. Overlap acts as a context bridge.

antipattern.description: Using the same chunk size for all document types.
antipattern.reason: Code, legal documents, and news articles have different optimal sizes. One-size-fits-all approaches sacrifice quality across all domains.
Fragments lose surrounding context and may not contain enough information to answer questions independently.
Dilutes relevance. Embedding models struggle with long texts. LLMs waste context budget on irrelevant sections.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A Claude subagent ingests a document corpus, selects the chunking strategy based on detected content type (Markdown headers present indicates MarkdownChunker, general prose indicates RecursiveChunker, sentences dominant indicates SentenceChunker), applies the chunker, and emits a list of {id, text, metadata} dicts to the next pipeline stage. The agent should emit a chunk-size distribution summary so a reviewing agent can flag outliers before the embedding step. Human review is only needed when p95 chunk size diverges more than 50 percent from the target.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent for orchestrating the chunking step within a broader RAG ingestion pipeline task. Prompt pattern: Given the document below, detect its content type, select the appropriate chunking strategy (fixed/sentence/paragraph/recursive), apply it with chunk_size equals 500 and overlap equals 50, and return a JSON list of {id, text, word_count, strategy_used} objects.

CLI tools: tiktoken for token-accurate fixed-size chunking. nltk for sentence tokenization. langchain-text-splitters for RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter and MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter. llama-index for node parsers. spacy for high-quality multilingual sentence splitting. Services: Unstructured.io for handling PDFs, DOCX, HTML before chunking. LlamaParse for structured extraction from PDFs with table preservation. Docling for local PDF/DOCX parsing with layout awareness.

Best practices: Always measure chunk token counts with tiktoken before indexing; word counts are 30 percent inaccurate. Set overlap to 10-20 percent of chunk_size; for QA corpora use 20 percent. Attach {source, page, chunk_index, strategy} metadata at creation time. Test chunking with five representative queries before committing. For mixed corpora, route each file type through its own chunker. Pre-clean text before chunking. Version the index name with the chunking config hash when strategy changes.

AI-agent gotchas: Agents writing chunk IDs as sequential integers break when documents are re-ingested; use md5(source_path + chunk_index) instead. NLTK punkt requires a download step; agents in sandboxed environments must pre-download. LLM-based chunking decisions add latency and cost that scale linearly with corpus size. When chunk_size changes mid-pipeline, existing vector index entries become stale. RecursiveChunker character-level overlap does not equal token-level overlap.

checklist.check: Read README.md for overview and key concepts
Check examples.md for real-world use cases
Add error handling for edge cases
Test with sample documents from at least 5 different content types
Measure token counts with tiktoken and verify overlap behavior
Benchmark chunk size distribution across sample documents
Run integration tests with embedding pipeline
Test retrieval quality with five representative queries
Document metadata schema for downstream pipeline consumers
Run chunking strategy on full document corpus
